w:r.w:t: AquaSol Backend · AI Engine · Database · Frontend

AquaSol is built as a Cyber-Physical System (CPS) where a digital twin in the cloud continuously mirrors and controls the physical irrigation hardware in the field. The system spans three distinct layers: the Farmer-Side Flutter application, the Cloud Backend intelligence engine, and the Hardware IoT layer consisting of LoRa-connected ESP32 nodes.
Cyber-Physical Digital Twin: Every field sensor reading is mirrored in the cloud database, enabling the backend to make decisions even when nodes temporarily go offline (Virtual Sensing mode).
Plugin Architecture: AI models live in plugins/ai/, hardware control in control/, keeping the codebase modular and testable independently.
HTTP Polling over MQTT: Chosen for its simplicity and reliability on weak 2G/3G rural signals. The Master Gateway polls for commands rather than maintaining a persistent connection.
MAC-Address Identity: Every device is uniquely identified by its hardware MAC address throughout the entire stack — firmware, backend, and database.

If a sensor node goes offline, the intelligence_engine.py does not halt irrigation decisions. Instead, it activates Virtual Sensing, simulating moisture loss over time using agronomic formulas based on:
Soil evaporation rate (determined by soil type — Loam, Clay, or Sand)
Crop transpiration coefficient (specific to crop type and growth stage)
Last known moisture reading as the starting baseline
This ensures continuous irrigation management even during temporary hardware failures, a key differentiator for the premium tier.

The Farm Diary module is not merely an activity log — it functions as a financial instrument. Every automated irrigation event triggered by the AI is tagged with is_subsidy=true in the database. This allows farmers to generate government-compliant reports for water-saving payout schemes, turning every irrigation decision into a potential subsidy claim.

The FastAPI backend is the central intelligence hub of AquaSol. It is stateless and horizontally scalable, with all persistent state stored in PostgreSQL. Its modular plugin architecture cleanly separates concerns:
All ML models: XGBoost, LSTM, Isolation Forest, RAG
Valve command queue, ACK processing, hardware handshake logic
FastAPI route definitions (sensors, auth, commands, crop planner)
Core 6-stage decision pipeline, called on every telemetry batch
SQLAlchemy ORM models mapping to PostgreSQL tables
All external communication enters through the FastAPI gateway. Key endpoints:
Receive telemetry batch from all nodes

Authentication uses JWT tokens. Every request from a paired ESP32 must include its device_secret (stored in NVS) or it receives a 403 Forbidden response. Mobile app requests use Bearer tokens obtained at login.
When the Master Gateway POSTs a telemetry batch to /api/v1/sensors/ingest, the backend performs the following steps in sequence:
Validate the master_secret against the database. Reject with 403 if invalid.
Unpack the events array. Each event contains a node_mac and its sensor readings.
Look up each node_mac in the Devices table to resolve its Zone and Farm.
For Node 2 (which has no DHT22 sensor), automatically copy the temperature and humidity values from Node 1 of the same zone.
Write all raw readings to the SensorReadings time-series table.
Trigger the intelligence_engine.py pipeline asynchronously for each affected zone.
Return the decisions JSON (valve commands) and any new_pairings in the HTTP response.

The AI service layer acts as an orchestrator between the raw data pipeline and the four ML models. It prepares feature vectors, calls models in the correct sequence, and aggregates results into a single IrrigationDecision object. Model calls are cached per zone for 60 seconds to prevent redundant inference on rapid telemetry bursts.
The Decision Engine translates the IrrigationDecision object into a concrete ValveCommand stored in the database. Key rules applied before a command is queued:
Escalate to farmer for manual decision

The Failsafe Service runs as a background worker, scanning all open valves every 60 seconds. If a valve has been open for longer than its configured max_irrigation_time (default 60 minutes) without receiving an ACK-close, it immediately queues a STOP_IRRIGATION command. This software-level safety net complements the hardware valve timeout ISR described in the firmware documentation.
The Actuator Service handles the reverse flow — translating backend decisions into LoRa commands forwarded to field nodes via the Master Gateway. Supported command actions are:
Open solenoid valve for the specified duration (seconds)
Close valve immediately — used for emergency stops and manual overrides
Change sleep interval or reporting frequency on the node
Perform a software reset of the target ESP32
Prepare the ESP32 to receive a new firmware image over-the-air

AquaSol uses a Robust HTTP Polling model, deliberately chosen over MQTT for reliability on weak rural 2G/3G signals. There are no persistent connections to maintain, and every exchange is a complete, self-contained HTTP transaction.
The Master Gateway does not forward data to the backend after every individual LoRa packet — this would waste bandwidth and power. Instead it uses a 30-second aggregation window:
Master starts a 30-second timer when the first LoRa packet arrives from any node.
During this window it collects packets from Node 1, Node 2, and any future nodes in the zone.
After the window closes (or all expected nodes have reported), it bundles everything into a single JSON batch and POSTs to /api/v1/sensors/ingest.

If the Master cannot reach the backend (WiFi down, server unreachable), it saves the accumulated batch to SPIFFS (ESP32 flash filesystem). When connectivity is restored, it replays all queued batches in chronological order, ensuring no sensor readings are permanently lost.
// Node 2 has no DHT22; backend copies temp from Node 1

On first boot, each ESP32 generates a random 6-digit pairing_code and stores it in NVS. Until paired, it includes pairing_code and master_secret: INITIAL_BOOT in every telemetry payload. The backend detects this, creates a device record, and returns a device_secret in the new_pairings array of the response. The ESP32 saves this secret to NVS and stops broadcasting the pairing code. Any subsequent request without the correct secret is rejected with 403.

AquaSol uses an ensemble of four distinct AI/ML models, each with a specific role in the irrigation decision pipeline. No single model makes the final call — they are layered so each compensates for the weaknesses of the others.

The XGBoost model is the primary decision-maker. It receives a fused feature vector of 12 inputs and outputs a binary irrigate/skip decision along with a confidence score. A confidence below 60% escalates the decision to the farmer for manual override rather than acting autonomously.

The LSTM (Long Short-Term Memory) model is a recurrent neural network trained on historical soil moisture time-series data. It predicts the moisture level 6 to 24 hours into the future, which the XGBoost engine uses as one of its 12 input features. This allows the system to make proactive decisions — for example, skipping an irrigation cycle now if the LSTM predicts moisture will remain sufficient through the night.
Historical sensor readings from DecisionLogs table

The Isolation Forest is an unsupervised model that detects sensor anomalies without requiring labeled training data. It works by randomly isolating data points — anomalous readings (spikes, stuck values, disconnected sensor noise) are isolated in fewer partitions than normal readings.
Spike anomaly: A single reading dramatically higher or lower than its neighbours (e.g., moisture jumping from 45% to 98% in one cycle).
Stuck sensor: Multiple consecutive identical readings indicating a frozen ADC or disconnected probe.
Out-of-range: Values outside physically plausible bounds (soil moisture > 100% or < 0%).

When an anomaly is flagged, the pipeline does not discard the reading silently. Instead, it activates Virtual Sensing, substituting the anomalous value with a physics-based estimate derived from the last known valid reading, soil type, and elapsed time.

The Agentic RAG (Retrieval-Augmented Generation) system is the natural language knowledge layer of AquaSol. It combines a FAISS vector index of ICAR (Indian Council of Agricultural Research) crop guides with the Llama 3.3 70B language model to produce agronomically accurate, human-readable advice.
Semantic index of ICAR crop guides and agronomic literature
Embeds user queries and finds relevant document chunks
Synthesises retrieved context into readable advice
Chains retrieval and generation, handles multi-step crop plans
Provides voice-enabled farmer interface to the RAG system

When a farmer requests a weekly crop plan, the RAG agent generates a structured 7-day task schedule specific to the crop type, current growth stage, and local weather context. Tasks include irrigation schedules, fertilisation timing, pest monitoring checkpoints, and harvest preparation steps.

The core function process_telemetry_and_decide in intelligence_engine.py runs every incoming telemetry batch through six sequential stages. All six must complete successfully before a valve command is queued.
Fetches real-time rain probability and humidity for the farm's GPS coordinates from the weather API. Result cached for 10 minutes per farm.
Fuses the raw sensor moisture reading with the LSTM model's prediction using a weighted average. This smooths out transient noise while preserving genuine trends.
Passes the fused reading through the Isolation Forest. If anomalous, replaces the value with a Virtual Sensing estimate and logs an alert to the farmer's app.
Estimates root zone moisture (e.g., at 30 cm depth) from the surface reading, using soil-type-specific percolation coefficients. This is the ecologically meaningful value for plant health.
Applies all agronomic guard rules: time-of-day, crop stage, rain probability, battery level, and confidence threshold. Any failed rule short-circuits the pipeline and logs an explanation.
Creates a ValveCommand row in the database with status PENDING. The Master Gateway retrieves this on its next HTTP poll cycle.
Primary relational database — stores all entities, configurations, and decision logs
PostgreSQL extension that converts SensorReadings into a hypertable for high-performance time-series queries and automatic chunk compression
Python ORM layer — maps database tables to Python model classes
Database migration tool — manages schema changes across deployments
Caching layer for AI model outputs and weather API responses (60-second TTL)
id, user_id, name, latitude, longitude, soil_type
Geographic container; soil_type: LOAM / CLAY / SAND
id, farm_id, name, crop_type, min_threshold, max_threshold
Irrigation block; thresholds define trigger moisture levels
id, zone_id, mac_address, device_type, is_paired, device_secret
id, device_id, soil_moisture, temperature, humidity, battery_pct, solar_pct, rain_detected, flow_rate, total_water, recorded_at
TimescaleDB hypertable; partitioned by recorded_at (weekly chunks)
id, zone_id, decision, confidence, explanation, weather_context, model_version, created_at
AI memory — every decision archived with its full reasoning
id, zone_id, device_id, action, duration_sec, status, created_at, acknowledged_at
id, farm_id, activity_type, notes, water_used_litres, is_subsidy, logged_at
Farmer activity journal; is_subsidy flags government-reportable events
id, zone_id, crop_type, sowing_date, current_stage, days_after_planting, created_at
Tracks crop lifecycle; current_stage: GERMINATION / VEGETATIVE / FLOWERING / FRUITING / HARVEST
id, crop_plan_id, week_number, task_type, description, due_date, is_completed
AI-generated task list from RAG crop planner

The SensorReadings table is the highest-volume table in the system, receiving a new row every 10 seconds per node. TimescaleDB is used to ensure query performance does not degrade as data accumulates.
SensorReadings partitioned into weekly chunks by recorded_at. Old chunks are compressed automatically after 7 days, reducing storage by ~90%.
TimescaleDB materialised views pre-compute hourly and daily averages for the dashboard, avoiding full table scans on every app load.
Composite index on (device_id, recorded_at DESC) — optimises the most common query pattern: latest N readings for a specific node.
Composite index on (zone_id, created_at DESC) in decision_logs — supports the AI pipeline's lookup of recent decisions for the feedback loop.
Partial index on valve_commands WHERE status = 'PENDING' — makes the polling endpoint O(1) for the common case of no pending commands.

The AquaSol mobile application is built with Flutter, enabling a single codebase to target both Android and iOS. State management uses the Provider package, which exposes observable data models to the widget tree. All backend communication is handled through the centralised ApiService class, which manages JWT token refresh, request retry logic, and offline queue management.
App-wide reactive state (farm data, decisions, alerts)
Backend calls, auth token management, error handling
Cached farm profile, user preferences, offline data
Live soil moisture per zone, AI decision status, valve controls, battery/solar indicators, rain alerts
Historical moisture chart (7/30/90 day), decision log with AI explanations, manual override toggle
AI-generated weekly task list, growth stage indicator, DAP counter, task completion tracking
Voice + text input, RAG-powered crop advice, multi-turn conversation, conversation history
Activity log, water usage summary, subsidy-eligible event filter, PDF report export
Node status (online/offline), battery level, solar efficiency, pairing QR code scanner
Push notifications for anomalies, low battery, valve failures, high rain probability
Threshold configuration per zone, irrigation time windows, notification preferences

Solu is the AI-powered farming assistant embedded in the app. It uses voice recognition to accept natural language queries from farmers and routes them to the Agentic RAG system on the backend. Responses are returned as structured text and optionally read aloud via text-to-speech, making it accessible to farmers with limited literacy.
"When should I irrigate my wheat?" — RAG retrieves ICAR wheat water requirements and cross-references current soil moisture.
"My soil looks dry but the app says 60%. Is it broken?" — Triggers an Isolation Forest check and explains the sensor calibration status.
"Generate a plan for this week." — Invokes the Crop Planner agent, returns a 7-day task list based on current growth stage.

The Crop Planner is a collaborative tool between the farmer and the AI. The farmer inputs the crop type and sowing date; the system automatically tracks the days after planting (DAP) and the current growth stage. The RAG agent then generates a 7-day plan tailored to that specific stage.
High frequency, low volume — keep topsoil consistently moist
Moderate — allow slight dry-down between cycles to encourage root depth
Critical stage — no moisture stress; AI confidence threshold tightened
Reduce volume; avoid waterlogging which causes root disease
Irrigation blocked by Policy Intelligence to ensure dry harvest conditions

AquaSol improves over time through a structured feedback loop. Every decision made by the AI is stored in the decision_logs table along with the sensor context at the time of the decision. A background worker runs weekly to retrain the LSTM model on the accumulated historical data, progressively improving forecast accuracy for each specific farm's microclimate.
Every Irrigate / Skip decision stored with 12-feature input vector, confidence score, and plain-language explanation.
Post-irrigation moisture readings captured 2 hours after valve close, providing ground-truth for model evaluation.
Weekly background job retrains LSTM on the farm's own historical data, personalising forecasts to local soil and weather patterns.
Monthly retraining with outcome labels (did irrigation improve moisture as expected?), improving decision accuracy over time.
When a farmer manually overrides an AI decision, this is logged as a training signal — the model learns from disagreements.
Isolation Forest contamination parameter auto-tuned monthly based on the false positive rate reported by farmers via the app.
The following issues were identified during system design and testing, and have been resolved in the current implementation:
Backend auto-copies temperature and humidity from Node 1 of the same zone during ingestion
Valve stays open on firmware crash
Firmware valve ISR forces GPIO low after 60 minutes, independent of main loop
No persistent storage of valve states
NVS Preferences library stores last valve state and learned threshold, restored on boot
TDMA slot system: Node 1 at +500ms, Node 2 at +2000ms after beacon
No anomaly detection on raw ADC values
Isolation Forest layer added; anomalies trigger Virtual Sensing fallback
No HTTP timeout on WiFi requests
5-second HTTP timeout + retry with exponential backoff; offline batches saved to SPIFFS
Crop stage not passed as XGBoost feature
Crop stage and DAP now feature 6 and 7 in the XGBoost input vector
Nodes toggling autonomous mode on/off rapidly
Anti-flapping thresholds: enter autonomous after 5 missed beacons, exit after 5 consecutive good beacons

The Stage Awareness Engine is the agronomic heart of AquaSol. Rather than treating a crop as a static entity with fixed water needs, it models the plant as a living organism moving through a biological lifecycle. Every irrigation decision is conditioned on which stage the crop is currently in — because the consequences of under- or over-watering change dramatically from germination to harvest.

AquaSol uses the FAO-56 methodology (Food and Agriculture Organization Irrigation and Drainage Paper No. 56) as the scientific foundation for all crop water requirement calculations. At the core of this methodology is the Crop Coefficient (Kc) — a dimensionless multiplier that scales the reference evapotranspiration (ETo) to the actual crop water demand.

The stage engine does not advance the crop through stages purely by calendar days. It checks biological readiness before allowing a transition. This is the key distinction from simple DAP-based timers.

If the soil moisture in a zone remains below the stress threshold for 5 or more consecutive days, the plant's physiological growth has been slowed by water deficit. The Stage Engine responds by holding the current stage — delaying the stage transition until conditions recover. This means the effective crop age (physiological stage) lags the calendar DAP, and the system adjusts all Kc values and moisture thresholds accordingly.
Moisture below stress threshold for 5+ days
Decision log scan over rolling 5-day window
Stage transition held; Kc values frozen at current stage; farmer alerted in app
Thermal Mitigation Mode activated; pulse irrigation schedule triggered
Stage transition resumes; physiological delay period logged in Diary
Yield estimation model confidence increases; P&L forecast updated

AquaSol ships with a database of stage maps for the most common crops (wheat, rice, cotton, sugarcane, tomato, maize, and others). However, when a farmer plants a crop not in the database, the system does not fail — it calls Groq AI to generate a scientifically accurate stage map in real time.
Farmer enters crop name in the app (e.g., 'Moringa' or 'Dragon Fruit').
Backend checks the crop_stage_library table. If not found, triggers Groq API call.
Groq returns a structured JSON stage map: stage names, DAP ranges, Kc values per stage, moisture thresholds, and water sensitivity flags.
Backend validates the returned values against agronomic bounds (e.g., Kc cannot exceed 1.4 for field crops).
Validated stage map is saved to crop_stage_library for future use and seeded into the zone's crop_plan.
"Generate a FAO-56 compliant growth stage map for [CROP_NAME]."
"Return JSON with: stage_name, dap_start, dap_end, kc_value,"
"Kc values must be agronomically validated. Respond ONLY with JSON."

When the Thermal Stress Index (TSI) — a composite of air temperature, VPD, and solar radiation — crosses a critical threshold, the system automatically enters Thermal Mitigation Mode. This mode overrides the normal irrigation schedule with pulse irrigation: short 2-minute bursts of water to cool the plant canopy, even if soil moisture is already within the acceptable range.
As calculated by Bio-Engine (20–60 min typical)
Every 45 minutes during peak heat hours (11:00–15:00)

When the weather API reports a rain probability at or above 90% for the coming 6 hours, the Stage Engine activates Eco Mode for the affected farm. In Eco Mode, all scheduled morning irrigation cycles are suppressed, allowing natural rainfall to fulfil the crop's water requirement. This decision and its weather justification are logged in the decision_logs table and displayed in the app with an explanation.
Irrigation proceeds; probability noted in decision explanation
Decision Engine reduces irrigation duration by 40%; farmer notified
Eco Mode activated; all irrigation skipped; diary entry created automatically

The Bio-Engine (bio_engine.py) is the most scientifically advanced module in AquaSol. While the AI models decide whether to irrigate, the Bio-Engine answers a deeper question: how much water does the plant actually need right now, and how should it be delivered? It models plant physiology using three interlocking calculations: Kc-based ETc, Vapour Pressure Deficit (VPD), and the Recovery Rate Controller (RRC).
The Bio-Engine calculates the Crop Evapotranspiration (ETc) — the theoretical volume of water the crop needs to consume per day — using the FAO-56 formula:

Where ETo (Reference Evapotranspiration) is derived from the Penman-Monteith equation using temperature, humidity, solar radiation, and wind speed data from the weather API. The result (in mm/day) is converted to litres per zone based on the zone's area, then divided across the day's irrigation cycles to determine burst volume.
Crop coefficient — changes with each growth stage
Reference ET for a standard grass reference crop
Used to convert mm/day to litres
Actual crop water requirement in litres/day

VPD measures the difference between the amount of moisture in the air and the maximum moisture the air can hold at the current temperature. A high VPD (hot, dry air) means the atmosphere is aggressively pulling water out of the plant's leaves through transpiration — even if the soil appears adequately moist. The Bio-Engine monitors VPD continuously and can trigger preventive irrigation before the soil moisture drops to the stress threshold.
No VPD-triggered action; normal ETc-based scheduling
ETc multiplied by 1.15; irrigation window brought forward by 1 hour
ETc multiplied by 1.35; preventive irrigation triggered regardless of soil moisture
Thermal Mitigation Mode activated; pulse irrigation + farmer alert

The RRC prevents osmotic shock — a physiological condition where roots are damaged by receiving a sudden large volume of water after a period of drought. When the soil moisture has been below the critical stress threshold (typically < 25%) for more than 48 hours, the RRC intervenes and breaks the required irrigation volume into a staged rehydration sequence.
Initial rehydration — allows roots to begin osmotic adjustment
Continued rehydration — soil now partially moist, roots can absorb safely
Final restoration — brings moisture to target level over 24-hour period

Each sip is issued as a separate ValveCommand in the database, scheduled at the appropriate time. The farmer's app shows the full rehydration sequence in the Zone Detail screen, labelled as RECOVERY MODE, so the farmer understands why the irrigation pattern looks different from normal.
Soil moisture below critical stress threshold (< 25%) for 48+ consecutive hours.
Total required ETc volume exceeds 2× the zone's standard burst volume.
Isolation Forest confirms the low reading is genuine (not a sensor anomaly).
Every time the Intelligence Pipeline reaches Stage 6 (Execution), it queries the Bio-Engine for the final irrigation parameters before writing the ValveCommand. The Bio-Engine returns:
Total water volume required for this irrigation event
Valve open time calculated from flow rate (L/min) and required volume
Whether to split into multi-sip RRC schedule
List of {time, volume_pct} objects if RRC is active
Whether Thermal Mitigation Mode overrides normal schedule
The ETc correction factor applied due to VPD (1.0 = no correction)
Full daily ETc for display in app and P&L calculation

The Farm Diary is the audit trail of AquaSol — the system's institutional memory. It records every significant event on the farm, whether triggered automatically by the AI or entered manually by the farmer. Its dual purpose is operational traceability (understanding exactly why each irrigation decision was made) and financial accountability (generating government-compliant subsidy reports).

Every time the AI pipeline completes an irrigation decision, it writes a structured diary entry automatically. The entry is deliberately written in plain language so that a farmer with limited technical background can understand it without needing to interpret raw sensor data.
"trigger": "Soil moisture dropped to 38% — below 42% threshold for Flowering stage.",
Farmers can log their own activities through the app. Manual entries are stored in the same diary_logs table and appear in the unified timeline alongside AI events. Supported manual activity types:
"Applied 5kg DAP fertilizer to Zone A via broadcast on Day 22"
Product name, target pest, dosage, zone
"Sprayed Chlorpyrifos 2ml/L for aphid infestation on Zone B"
"Observed waterlogging near Row 4. Reduced irrigation threshold."
"Harvested 820 kg from Zone A. Grade A. Sent to mandi."

In many Indian states and other agricultural regions, governments offer financial incentives for precision irrigation — payments to farmers who can demonstrate that they irrigated scientifically rather than by habit. AquaSol's diary is designed to satisfy these audit requirements automatically.
An entry is tagged as is_subsidy = true (a Verified Precision Event) when all of the following conditions are met:
The irrigation was triggered by the AI pipeline (not a manual override).
The decision included a weather context check (rain probability verified).
A flow sensor reading confirmed the actual volume delivered.

VPEs form the basis of the subsidy report export. The report aggregates all VPEs across a date range and computes the total verified water savings compared to a conventional flood-irrigation baseline (typically 6,000 L/acre/day for rice, 4,000 L/acre/day for wheat).

In the mobile app, the Farm Diary is displayed as a unified chronological timeline, mixing AI events and manual entries. Each entry is colour-coded by type and expandable to show the full detail including the AI's plain-language explanation. Farmers can filter by zone, date range, activity type, or subsidy-eligible events only.

The P&L module converts the raw operational data collected across the season into financial intelligence. It answers the question every farmer ultimately cares about: is this crop profitable? By combining water usage logs, input costs from the diary, yield estimates, and real-time market prices, it gives the farmer a live economic picture of their crop's performance — not just at harvest, but throughout the growing season.

Every litre of water pumped has a cost — electricity for the pump motor, or fuel if diesel-powered. AquaSol calculates this cost automatically from the zone's pump configuration entered during farm setup.
Calculate power draw in kW: kW = HP × 0.746
Cost per hour of pump runtime

All manual diary entries with a financial component (fertilizer, pesticides, labour, maintenance) are aggregated into the Input Cost ledger. Each entry can optionally include a unit cost; the system sums these across the season per zone and per farm to produce a complete cost-of-production figure.

The yield estimator runs as a background calculation, updated daily. It uses two inputs: the crop's theoretical maximum yield (from the Groq/RAG crop database) and the Optimal Days count — the number of days during the current season where soil moisture was maintained within the crop's ideal band.

This estimate is intentionally conservative — it does not account for pest damage or soil nutrient deficiencies (though diary-logged pest events do apply a configurable yield penalty). The estimate is displayed in the P&L screen with a confidence band that widens in early stages and narrows as the crop approaches harvest.
The P&L module fetches the current market price (mandi price) for the crop via the AI/RAG layer, which queries commodity price APIs. Revenue is estimated as:
Running total, updated after each irrigation
Sum of manual diary cost entries
Sum of manual diary cost entries
Mandi price API via RAG layer
VPE count × government rate per VPE

The P&L screen is zone-level by default, with a farm-level rollup tab that aggregates across all zones. This is important for farms growing different crops in different zones — each zone has its own independent P&L, and the farm summary shows the combined picture.

The Reports module generates exportable documents from the data accumulated in the diary_logs, decision_logs, sensor_readings, and valve_commands tables. All reports are generated server-side as PDF files and delivered to the farmer's mobile app for sharing or printing. Reports can be scoped to a specific zone, the whole farm, or a custom date range.
All Verified Precision Events (VPEs), total water saved vs. conventional baseline, XGBoost confidence scores, flow sensor verification
Daily/weekly/monthly water consumption per zone, flow rate trends, anomaly events, comparison to ETc targets
Full cost breakdown, yield estimate vs. actual (post-harvest), ROI, subsidy credits claimed
Stage progression log, Optimal Days count, yield estimate history, harvest log entry
All decisions with 12-feature input vectors, model versions, confidence scores, and plain-language explanations
Battery trends, solar efficiency, signal strength history, anomaly flags, calibration drift indicators

The subsidy report is the highest-stakes document AquaSol generates. It must meet the evidentiary standards required for government payout schemes, so each VPE entry includes the full chain of evidence:
Timestamp of the irrigation event (UTC and local time).
Zone identifier and GPS coordinates of the farm.
Soil moisture reading that triggered the decision (raw value + calibrated %).
Weather context at the time: temperature, humidity, rain probability.
XGBoost decision with confidence score and the 12-feature input vector.
Flow sensor confirmation: actual volume delivered in litres.
Comparison to conventional baseline: litres saved per event.
Cumulative season water savings and equivalent cost savings.
The report is digitally structured (not just a formatted table) so it can be submitted electronically to government portals that accept structured precision irrigation data.

Reports are generated asynchronously via a background job to avoid blocking the API. The farmer requests a report through the app; the backend queues a report_generation_job and sends a push notification when ready.
Farmer taps 'Generate Report' in app with date range + report type
Backend creates a report_jobs row with status QUEUED
Background worker picks up job, queries relevant tables
Data formatted into structured report object
PDF stored in object storage (S3-compatible); signed URL generated
Push notification sent to farmer's device with download link
Farmer opens PDF in app or shares via WhatsApp / email

All sensor readings are retained at full resolution for 90 days. After 90 days, TimescaleDB continuous aggregates compress data to hourly averages, retaining these for 2 years. Decision logs and diary entries are retained at full resolution indefinitely, as they are the legal record of farm activity. Report PDFs are stored for 1 year from generation date.
